schools.summary: Balaam ceasing from enchantments and turning to the wilderness: 'and when Balaam saw that it pleased the LORD to bless Israel, he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness' — seeing that God would bless Israel, Balaam ceased seeking omens and looked toward the camp (the it pleased the LORD to bless Israel, God's purpose to bless, twice declared, the he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments, Balaam ceasing from divination, the he set his face toward the wilderness, turning to the camp of Israel, the figure, the diviner forced to own God's purpose, the enchantments useless against the blessing, the truth that no divination prevails against God's people, 'there is no enchantment against Jacob,' Numbers 23:23, the figure of the powerlessness of all sorcery before God's blessing, Balaam now waiting for the Spirit rather than seeking omens, vv.2); Balaam seeing God's will to bless (the purpose owned, Numbers 23:23) — the blessing (God's purpose); 'he went not... to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness' (Balaam ceasing from divination, turning to the camp, the enchantments useless, the figure, no sorcery against the blessing, the providence, the change, the Spirit coming upon him, vv.2) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And when Balaam saw that it pleased the LORD to bless Israel, he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness
Balaam ceasing from enchantments and turning to the wilderness
the diviner forced to own God's purpose to bless, the enchantments useless against the blessing
no sorcery prevailing against God's people (Numbers 23:23)

schools.summary: The Spirit of God coming upon Balaam as he saw Israel encamped: 'and Balaam lifted up his eyes, and he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes; and the spirit of God came upon him' — beholding Israel orderly encamped by tribes, the Spirit of God came upon Balaam to prophesy (the he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes, the orderly camp, the tribes in array, the the spirit of God came upon him, the Spirit moving the prophet, the figure, the Spirit of God using even Balaam to bless Israel, the unwilling prophet moved to true prophecy, the orderly camp a goodly sight, the tribes in their God-appointed order, the truth that God put his word even in the mouth of the hireling diviner, the Spirit overruling, the figure of the sovereign Spirit who speaks through whom he will, the blessing coming by the Spirit, not by enchantment); Israel orderly encamped by tribes (the goodly sight) — the camp (the tribes in order); 'the spirit of God came upon him' (the Spirit moving Balaam to prophesy, the prophet overruled, the figure, the sovereign Spirit speaking through whom he will, the providence, the Spirit, the third oracle to follow, vv.3-9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Balaam lifted up his eyes, and he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes; and the spirit of God came upon him
the Spirit of God coming upon Balaam as he saw Israel encamped
the Spirit moving even the hireling diviner to true prophecy, the blessing coming by the Spirit
God speaking through whom he will, not by enchantment

schools.summary: The opening of Balaam's third oracle: 'and he took up his parable, and said, Balaam the son of Beor hath said, and the man whose eyes are open hath said' — Balaam began his oracle, declaring himself the man whose eyes were opened to see (the he took up his parable, the prophetic oracle begun, the Balaam the son of Beor hath said, the speaker named, the the man whose eyes are open hath said, the opened eyes, the prophetic vision, the figure, the prophet whose eyes were opened by God to see the truth, though against his will, the irony that Balaam, who would not see the angel, Numbers 22:31, now has his eyes opened to behold Israel's blessing, the truth that God opens the eyes of whom he will to see his purpose, the figure of the seer, the opened eyes that behold the vision of the Almighty, vv.4); Balaam taking up his parable (the oracle begun) — the parable (the oracle); 'the man whose eyes are open hath said' (the prophetic vision, the eyes opened, Numbers 22:31, the figure, God opening the eyes to see his purpose, the providence, the opened eyes, the vision of the Almighty to follow, vv.4) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And he took up his parable, and said, Balaam the son of Beor hath said, and the man whose eyes are open hath said
the opening of Balaam's third oracle
Balaam declaring himself the seer whose eyes God had opened to behold the truth
God opening the eyes of whom he will (Numbers 22:31)

schools.summary: Balaam hearing God's words and seeing the vision of the Almighty: 'he hath said, which heard the words of God, which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open' — Balaam claimed to hear God's words and see the vision of the Almighty, in a trance with eyes opened (the which heard the words of God, the words received, the which saw the vision of the Almighty, the vision, the falling into a trance, but having his eyes open, the prophetic ecstasy, the opened eyes, the figure, the prophet hearing and seeing by God's gift, the vision of the Almighty given even to Balaam, the truth that the words and the vision were God's, not the diviner's own, the figure of the revelation that comes from God, the prophet but the mouth, the irony of the opened eyes given to the covetous seer, that he might bless whom he would have cursed); Balaam hearing God's words (the revelation received) — the words (heard of God); 'which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open' (the vision and the trance, the prophetic ecstasy, the figure, the revelation from God, the prophet the mouth, the providence, the vision, the blessing of the tents to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: He hath said, which heard the words of God, which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open
Balaam hearing God's words and seeing the vision of the Almighty
the words and the vision God's own, given even to the diviner, the prophet but the mouth
the opened eyes that he might bless whom he would have cursed

schools.summary: Balaam's praise of the beauty of Israel's dwellings: 'how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, and thy tabernacles, O Israel!' — the beauty of the tents — 'how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, and thy tabernacles, O Israel': Balaam, beholding the orderly camp, broke out in praise of the beauty of Israel's dwellings (the witnesses noting the significance — the goodliness of God's people in their order and blessing, the camp of Israel, set in array by their tribes around the tabernacle, a goodly and beautiful sight, the favour of God upon them visible, the lesson — the beauty of the church of God, the dwellings of his people made comely by his presence, the tents goodly not in themselves but as the dwelling of a people God had blessed, the order, the blessing, the presence of God among them their true beauty, the truth that the unwilling prophet was made to praise what he came to curse, owning the comeliness God had given, the figure — the beauty of the church, 'the king's daughter is all glorious within,' Psalm 45:13, the comeliness God puts upon his people, 'perfect through my comeliness, which I had put upon thee,' Ezekiel 16:14, the dwellings of God's people made goodly by his favour, a figure of the beauty of the church in the eyes of God, 'how amiable are thy tabernacles, O LORD of hosts,' Psalm 84:1, the truth that God's people are beautiful in his sight, the camp of the redeemed a goodly thing, the figure of the goodliness of the dwellings of God's blessed people); the orderly camp, the tents in array (the beauty of the ordered camp, Psalm 84:1) — the beauty (the goodly tents); the dwellings made comely by God's blessing and presence (the beauty of God's people, the favour upon them, Psalm 45:13; Ezekiel 16:14) — the figure (the beauty of the church, the comeliness God gives, the providence, the praise, the blessing of fruitfulness to follow, vv.6-7) — Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'how goodly are thy tents' celebrate?

sides.position: The beauty of God's people in their order and blessing, the dwellings made comely not in themselves but by the blessing and presence of God - the unwilling prophet made to praise what he came to curse, a figure of the beauty of the church made glorious by the comeliness God puts upon her (Psalm 45:13; Ezekiel 16:14; Psalm 84:1)

themes: How goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, and thy tabernacles, O Israel!
Balaam's praise of the beauty of Israel's dwellings (the crux of the goodliness of God's people)
the dwellings made goodly not in themselves but by the blessing and presence of God
a figure of the beauty of the church made glorious by the comeliness God puts upon her (Psalm 45:13; Ezekiel 16:14; Psalm 84:1)

schools.summary: The images of Israel's fruitfulness and beauty: 'as the valleys are they spread forth, as gardens by the river's side, as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted, and as cedar trees beside the waters' — Balaam likened Israel to spreading valleys, watered gardens, fragrant aloes, and cedars by the waters (the as the valleys are they spread forth, the wide and flourishing people, the as gardens by the river's side, the watered, fruitful garden, the as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted, the fragrant trees, God's planting, the as cedar trees beside the waters, the lofty, enduring cedars, the figure, the images of fruitfulness, beauty, and permanence, Israel flourishing as God's planting, the truth that God's people are like trees planted by the waters, fruitful and enduring, 'he shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water,' Psalm 1:3, the figure of the church watered by God, the trees the LORD hath planted, 'that they might be called trees of righteousness, the planting of the LORD,' Isaiah 61:3, the fragrance and fruitfulness of God's blessed people); Israel as spreading valleys and watered gardens (the fruitfulness, Psalm 1:3) — the fruitfulness (the flourishing people); 'the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted... cedar trees beside the waters' (the fragrant aloes and lofty cedars, God's planting, the trees of righteousness, Isaiah 61:3, the figure, the church watered and planted by God, the providence, the images, the blessing of the king to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: As the valleys are they spread forth, as gardens by the river's side, as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted, and as cedar trees beside the waters
the images of Israel's fruitfulness and beauty
Israel like watered gardens and cedars by the waters, fruitful and enduring
the trees the LORD hath planted, the church watered and made fruitful by God (Psalm 1:3; Isaiah 61:3)

schools.summary: The blessing of abundant seed and an exalted king: 'he shall pour the water out of his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters, and his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted' — Balaam foretold Israel's abundant increase and an exalted king and kingdom (the he shall pour the water out of his buckets, the abundance of water and increase, the his seed shall be in many waters, the numerous offspring, the his king shall be higher than Agag, the king above the Amalekite king, the his kingdom shall be exalted, the kingdom raised on high, the figure, the blessing of increase and dominion, the king and kingdom exalted, the foreshadowing of Israel's kingdom, and beyond it the kingdom of the Messiah, the King higher than all, the truth that God would exalt his people's king and kingdom, the figure of the kingdom of Christ exalted above all, 'his kingdom shall be exalted,' a kingdom that shall not be destroyed, Daniel 2:44, the abundant seed a figure of the multitude of God's people); the abundance of water and numerous seed (the increase) — the increase (the abundant seed); 'his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted' (the exalted king and kingdom, the dominion, the kingdom of the Messiah, Daniel 2:44, the figure, the kingdom of Christ exalted above all, the providence, the blessing, the strength of Israel to follow, vv.8-9) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: He shall pour the water out of his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters, and his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted
the blessing of abundant seed and an exalted king
Israel's abundant increase and exalted king, a foreshadowing of the kingdom of the Messiah
the King higher than all, a kingdom that shall not be destroyed (Daniel 2:44)

schools.summary: The strength of Israel brought out of Egypt: 'God brought him forth out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn: he shall eat up the nations his enemies, and shall break their bones, and pierce them through with his arrows' — God who brought Israel out of Egypt gave them the strength of the wild ox to overcome their enemies (the God brought him forth out of Egypt, the redemption from Egypt, the ground of strength, the he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn, the strength of the wild ox, the he shall eat up the nations his enemies... break their bones... pierce them through, the victory over the foe, the figure, the strength God gave his people to overcome the nations, the truth that the redemption from Egypt was the pledge of victory, God's power their strength, the figure of the conquering people, strong in God to subdue the enemies, the strength not their own but God's, who brought them out and would give them the land, the victory of God's people over their foes by his power); Israel's strength from God who redeemed them (the strength of the wild ox) — the strength (from God); 'he shall eat up the nations his enemies... pierce them through' (the conquest of the foe, the victory by God's strength, the figure, the conquering people strong in God, the providence, the strength, the lion's rest to follow, vv.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: God brought him forth out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn: he shall eat up the nations his enemies, and shall break their bones, and pierce them through with his arrows
the strength of Israel brought out of Egypt
God who redeemed Israel from Egypt giving them strength to overcome the nations
the victory not their own but God's

schools.summary: The lion at rest and the Abrahamic blessing reaffirmed: 'he couched, he lay down as a lion, and as a great lion: who shall stir him up? Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee' — the reaffirmed promise — 'Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee': Balaam, describing Israel as a lion at rest none dare rouse, pronounced the very blessing God had given Abraham (the witnesses noting the significance — the Abrahamic promise put in the mouth of the hireling prophet, the blessing first spoken to Abraham, 'I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee,' Genesis 12:3, now reaffirmed over Israel by the very man hired to curse them, the lesson — the inviolable security of God's people, Israel as a lion couched, whom none dare stir up, safe under God's protection, the blessing and the curse turned on those who deal with them, to bless them is to be blessed, to curse them is to be cursed, the irony — Balaam, sent to curse, compelled to bless and to pronounce a curse on all who would curse Israel, including himself and Balak, the figure — the security of the church under God, 'no weapon that is formed against thee shall prosper,' Isaiah 54:17, the people whom God blesses cannot be cursed, 'what God hath blessed, none can reverse,' the truth that the blessing of Abraham rests on Israel and on all who are blessed in his Seed, 'in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed,' Genesis 12:3; Galatians 3:8-9, the figure of the inviolable favour of God upon his people, the lion at rest none can rouse, the figure of the blessing that cannot be reversed and the people that cannot be cursed); Israel as a lion at rest, whom none dare rouse (the security under God, the strength at rest) — the lion (the security of God's people); 'Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee' (the very blessing of Abraham reaffirmed, the blessing and curse turned on those who deal with Israel, the inviolable favour, Genesis 12:3; Galatians 3:8-9, the figure, the church that cannot be cursed, Isaiah 54:17, the providence, the blessing, Balak's anger to follow, vv.10) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the reaffirmed 'Blessed is he that blesseth thee' establish?

sides.position: The very Abrahamic promise (Genesis 12:3) put in the mouth of the hireling prophet - the inviolable security of God's people whom none can curse, the blessing and curse turned on all who deal with them, a figure of the favour of God upon his church (Isaiah 54:17) and the blessing of Abraham resting on all blessed in his Seed (Galatians 3:8-9)

themes: He couched, he lay down as a lion, and as a great lion: who shall stir him up? Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee
the lion at rest and the Abrahamic blessing reaffirmed (the crux of the inviolable blessing of God's people)
the very promise to Abraham put in the mouth of the hireling prophet, the security of God's people whom none can curse
a figure of the inviolable favour of God upon his church and the blessing of Abraham in his Seed (Genesis 12:3; Galatians 3:8-9; Isaiah 54:17)

schools.summary: Balak's anger that Balaam had blessed Israel: 'and Balak's anger was kindled against Balaam, and he smote his hands together: and Balak said unto Balaam, I called thee to curse mine enemies, and, behold, thou hast altogether blessed them these three times' — Balak, enraged, struck his hands together and rebuked Balaam for blessing Israel thrice (the Balak's anger was kindled... he smote his hands together, the rage, the I called thee to curse mine enemies, the purpose of the hiring, the thou hast altogether blessed them these three times, the threefold blessing, the figure, the frustrated rage of the enemy, unable to curse God's people, the truth that the purpose of the wicked against God's people is overruled and turned to blessing, the figure of the enemy's vain fury, the curse turned to blessing three times over, the powerlessness of all the malice of men against those God has blessed, the rage of Balak vain); Balak's fury at the blessing (the rage) — the wrath (Balak enraged); 'thou hast altogether blessed them these three times' (the curse turned to blessing thrice, the purpose overruled, the figure, the malice of the enemy vain, the providence, the rage, Balaam's defense to follow, vv.11-13) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And Balak's anger was kindled against Balaam, and he smote his hands together: and Balak said unto Balaam, I called thee to curse mine enemies, and, behold, thou hast altogether blessed them these three times
Balak's anger that Balaam had blessed Israel
Balak's rage at the curse turned to blessing thrice over, the malice of the enemy vain
the purpose of the wicked overruled and turned to blessing

schools.summary: Balak dismissing Balaam, blaming the LORD for the lost honour: 'therefore now flee thou to thy place: I thought to promote thee unto great honour; but, lo, the LORD hath kept thee back from honour' — Balak angrily dismissed Balaam, taunting that the LORD had kept him from the promised reward (the now flee thou to thy place, the dismissal, the I thought to promote thee unto great honour, the promised reward, the the LORD hath kept thee back from honour, the reward lost, blamed on God, the figure, the wages of unrighteousness withheld, the diviner's covetousness disappointed, the irony that Balak blames the LORD, who indeed kept Balaam from the reward of cursing, the truth that the covetous prophet, who loved the wages of unrighteousness, 2 Peter 2:15, is denied them, the figure of the gain of wickedness frustrated, the LORD keeping his own from being cursed and the hireling from his hire); Balak dismissing Balaam, the reward withheld (the lost honour) — the dismissal (Balaam sent away); 'the LORD hath kept thee back from honour' (the reward denied by the LORD, the covetousness disappointed, 2 Peter 2:15, the figure, the wages of unrighteousness withheld, the providence, the reward lost, Balaam's defense to follow, vv.12-13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Therefore now flee thou to thy place: I thought to promote thee unto great honour; but, lo, the LORD hath kept thee back from honour
Balak dismissing Balaam, blaming the LORD for the lost honour
the covetous diviner denied his reward, the LORD keeping the hireling from his hire
the gain of wickedness frustrated (2 Peter 2:15)

schools.summary: Balaam reminding Balak of his warning: 'and Balaam said unto Balak, Spake I not also to thy messengers which thou sentest unto me, saying' — Balaam answered, recalling that he had warned Balak's messengers from the first (vv.13, the Spake I not also to thy messengers, the reminder of the warning, the which thou sentest unto me, the embassy from Balak, the figure, Balaam defending himself, recalling that he had told the messengers he could speak only God's word, the truth that even the covetous prophet had to own he could not go beyond God's word, the figure of the diviner's helplessness, bound to speak only what God gave, the warning Balaam had given, now recalled to clear himself, vv.13); Balaam recalling his warning to the messengers (the defense) — the reminder (the warning recalled); 'which thou sentest unto me' (the messengers from Balak, the embassy, the figure, Balaam bound to God's word, the providence, the defense, the words of the warning to follow, vv.13) — Gill.

themes: And Balaam said unto Balak, Spake I not also to thy messengers which thou sentest unto me, saying
Balaam reminding Balak of his warning
Balaam recalling that he had warned the messengers he could speak only what God gave
the covetous prophet bound to God's word

schools.summary: Balaam owning he could speak only God's word: 'if Balak would give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the commandment of the LORD, to do either good or bad of mine own mind; but what the LORD saith, that will I speak?' — Balaam owned that no reward could make him go beyond God's word (the if Balak would give me his house full of silver and gold, the greatest reward, the I cannot go beyond the commandment of the LORD, the diviner bound, the what the LORD saith, that will I speak, only God's word spoken, the figure, the prophet bound to God's word, unable to curse whom God had blessed, the truth that no bribe can overrule God's purpose, the word of the LORD alone prevailing, the irony that the covetous Balaam, who loved the wages, must yet own he could not earn them by cursing, the figure of the sovereignty of God's word over all the schemes and bribes of men, the blessing of God's people that no gold can reverse); Balaam bound by God's command, no reward availing (the word of the LORD) — the binding (no bribe prevailing); 'what the LORD saith, that will I speak' (only God's word spoken, the sovereignty of the word, the figure, the blessing no gold can reverse, the providence, the confession, the fourth oracle to follow, vv.14-25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If Balak would give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the commandment of the LORD, to do either good or bad of mine own mind; but what the LORD saith, that will I speak?
Balaam owning he could speak only God's word
the diviner bound to God's word, no reward availing to overrule God's purpose
the blessing of God's people that no gold can reverse

schools.summary: Balaam announcing the prophecy of the latter days: 'and now, behold, I go unto my people: come therefore, and I will advertise thee what this people shall do to thy people in the latter days' — before departing, Balaam offered to foretell what Israel would do to Moab and the nations in the latter days (the now, behold, I go unto my people, Balaam's departure, the I will advertise thee what this people shall do to thy people, the warning of Israel's future dealing with Moab, the in the latter days, the time of fulfillment, the messianic future, the figure, the prophecy of the latter days, the distant future opened, the truth that even the hireling prophet was made to foretell the triumph of God's people and the coming of the King, the figure of the prophecy reaching to the latter days, the messianic age, the Star and Sceptre to come, vv.17, the unwilling seer announcing the far future of God's purpose); Balaam about to depart, offering the prophecy (the announcement) — the departure (Balaam going); 'what this people shall do to thy people in the latter days' (the prophecy of the messianic future, the latter days, the figure, the far future of God's purpose, the King to come, the providence, the announcement, the Star out of Jacob to follow, vv.15-17) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And now, behold, I go unto my people: come therefore, and I will advertise thee what this people shall do to thy people in the latter days
Balaam announcing the prophecy of the latter days
the unwilling prophet foretelling the triumph of God's people and the coming of the King
the prophecy reaching to the messianic age

schools.summary: The opening of Balaam's fourth oracle: 'and he took up his parable, and said, Balaam the son of Beor hath said, and the man whose eyes are open hath said' — Balaam began his fourth and greatest oracle, again declaring himself the seer whose eyes were opened (the he took up his parable, the oracle begun, the Balaam the son of Beor hath said, the speaker, the the man whose eyes are open hath said, the opened eyes, the prophetic sight, the figure, the prophet's opened eyes, the same solemn preface as the third oracle, vv.3, the seer about to behold the far future, the Star and Sceptre, the truth that God opens the eyes of his chosen mouth to see the vision, the figure of the prophetic sight given even to Balaam, that he might foretell the King, the solemn opening of the greatest of the oracles); Balaam taking up his parable (the oracle) — the parable (the oracle begun); 'the man whose eyes are open hath said' (the prophetic sight, the eyes opened, the seer, vv.3, the figure, God opening the eyes to behold the vision, the providence, the opened eyes, the vision of the Almighty to follow, vv.16) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And he took up his parable, and said, Balaam the son of Beor hath said, and the man whose eyes are open hath said
the opening of Balaam's fourth oracle
Balaam again declaring himself the seer whose eyes God had opened, about to behold the far future
the Star and Sceptre to come

schools.summary: Balaam hearing God's words and knowing the knowledge of the most High: 'he hath said, which heard the words of God, and knew the knowledge of the most High, which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open' — Balaam claimed to hear God's words, know the knowledge of the most High, and see the vision of the Almighty (the which heard the words of God, the words received, the and knew the knowledge of the most High, the knowledge given, fuller than the third oracle, the which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open, the vision and the ecstasy, the figure, the prophet given the knowledge of the most High, the fuller revelation of the fourth oracle, the truth that God gave even Balaam to know his purpose, that he might foretell the King, the figure of the revelation of God's knowledge, the seer beholding the far future, the knowledge of the most High given that the Star and Sceptre be foretold, vv.17); Balaam knowing the knowledge of the most High (the fuller revelation) — the knowledge (of the most High); 'which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open' (the vision and the ecstasy, the prophetic sight, the figure, the seer beholding the far future, the providence, the vision, the Star out of Jacob to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott.

themes: He hath said, which heard the words of God, and knew the knowledge of the most High, which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open
Balaam knowing the knowledge of the most High
the prophet given to know God's purpose, the fuller revelation of the fourth oracle
that the Star and Sceptre be foretold

schools.summary: The prophecy of the Star out of Jacob and the Sceptre out of Israel: 'I shall see him, but not now: I shall behold him, but not nigh: there shall come a Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre shall rise out of Israel, and shall smite the corners of Moab, and destroy all the children of Sheth' — the Messiah foretold — 'there shall come a Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre shall rise out of Israel': Balaam, beholding the far future, foretold a Star and a Sceptre to arise from Israel, who would conquer Moab and the nations (the witnesses noting the significance — the coming of the Messiah, the King of God's people, the Star and the Sceptre images of a glorious King to arise out of Jacob, the ruler who would have dominion, the lesson — the great messianic prophecy, fulfilled first in David, who smote Moab, but supremely in Christ, the true King of Israel, the Star, the bright and morning star, 'I am the root and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star,' Revelation 22:16, the Sceptre, the royal ruler, 'the sceptre shall not depart from Judah... until Shiloh come,' Genesis 49:10, the 'not now... not nigh' — the King seen afar, the distant fulfillment of the latter days, the conquest of Moab and the children of Sheth a figure of the subduing of all the enemies of God's kingdom under the Messiah's rule, the figure — Christ the King who arises out of Israel, the Star whose rising the wise men beheld, 'we have seen his star in the east,' Matthew 2:2, the Sceptre of his everlasting kingdom, 'a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom,' Hebrews 1:8, the truth that the hireling diviner was made to prophesy the coming of Christ, the King who would rule and conquer, the figure of the Messiah, the Star and Sceptre, the King of God's people who subdues all his foes, the figure of the coming King who arises out of Jacob to reign); the King seen afar, the distant fulfillment (not now, not nigh, the latter days) — the distance (the King afar); 'there shall come a Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre shall rise out of Israel, and shall smite the corners of Moab, and destroy all the children of Sheth' (the Star and Sceptre, the King arising to rule and conquer, fulfilled in David and supremely in Christ, the Messiah, the bright morning star, Genesis 49:10; Revelation 22:16; Matthew 2:2, the figure, the King of God's people who subdues all foes, Hebrews 1:8, the providence, the prophecy, the oracles against the nations to follow, vv.18-24) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does the Star out of Jacob and the Sceptre out of Israel signify?

sides.position: The coming of the Messiah, the glorious King to arise out of Jacob - fulfilled first in David who smote Moab, but supremely in Christ the true King of Israel, the bright and morning star whose rising the wise men beheld (Genesis 49:10; Revelation 22:16; Matthew 2:2), the Sceptre of the everlasting kingdom who subdues all the enemies of God's people (Hebrews 1:8)

themes: There shall come a Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre shall rise out of Israel, and shall smite the corners of Moab, and destroy all the children of Sheth
the prophecy of the Star out of Jacob and the Sceptre out of Israel (the crux of the coming of the Messiah, the King of God's people)
the glorious King to arise out of Jacob, fulfilled first in David but supremely in Christ the true King of Israel
the bright and morning star and the Sceptre of the everlasting kingdom who subdues all his foes (Genesis 49:10; Revelation 22:16; Matthew 2:2; Hebrews 1:8)

schools.summary: The conquest of Edom and Seir by Israel: 'and Edom shall be a possession, Seir also shall be a possession for his enemies; and Israel shall do valiantly' — Balaam foretold that Edom and Seir would become Israel's possession, Israel prevailing (the Edom shall be a possession, Edom subdued, the Seir also shall be a possession for his enemies, Seir, Edom's mountain, conquered, the Israel shall do valiantly, Israel's valour and victory, the figure, the conquest of Edom, the old enemy, by Israel, the foreshadowing of David's subduing of Edom, 2 Samuel 8:14, and beyond it the subduing of all the enemies of God's kingdom under the Messiah, the truth that the foes of God's people are given into their hand, the figure of the victory of God's people over their enemies, Israel doing valiantly by God's strength, the kingdom prevailing); Edom and Seir subdued (the conquest, 2 Samuel 8:14) — the conquest (Edom possessed); 'Israel shall do valiantly' (Israel's victory by God's strength, the kingdom prevailing, the figure, the foes subdued under the Messiah, the providence, the conquest, the dominion of the King to follow, vv.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Edom shall be a possession, Seir also shall be a possession for his enemies; and Israel shall do valiantly
the conquest of Edom and Seir by Israel
Edom the old enemy subdued by Israel, fulfilled in David and foreshadowing the subduing of all foes under the Messiah
Israel doing valiantly by God's strength (2 Samuel 8:14)

schools.summary: The dominion of the ruler who comes out of Jacob: 'out of Jacob shall come he that shall have dominion, and shall destroy him that remaineth of the city' — the prophecy returns to the King out of Jacob, who would have dominion and destroy the remnant of the foe (the out of Jacob shall come he that shall have dominion, the ruler from Jacob, the King of vv.17, the and shall destroy him that remaineth of the city, the subduing of the last of the enemy, the figure, the dominion of the King out of Jacob, the Star and Sceptre of vv.17 now the ruler who reigns, the foreshadowing of David's dominion, and supremely the universal dominion of Christ, the truth that the King out of Jacob shall rule over all and subdue all his foes, the figure of the dominion of the Messiah, 'he shall have dominion also from sea to sea,' Psalm 72:8, 'of the increase of his government... there shall be no end,' Isaiah 9:7, the King who reigns and destroys the last of the enemy); the King out of Jacob who has dominion (the ruler, Psalm 72:8) — the dominion (the King's rule); 'shall destroy him that remaineth of the city' (the last of the foe subdued, the dominion complete, Isaiah 9:7, the figure, the universal dominion of Christ, the providence, the dominion, the oracles against the nations to follow, vv.20-24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Out of Jacob shall come he that shall have dominion, and shall destroy him that remaineth of the city
the dominion of the ruler who comes out of Jacob
the King out of Jacob who has dominion and subdues the last of the foe
a figure of the universal dominion of Christ (Psalm 72:8; Isaiah 9:7)

schools.summary: The oracle against Amalek, doomed to perish: 'and when he looked on Amalek, he took up his parable, and said, Amalek was the first of the nations; but his latter end shall be that he perish for ever' — Balaam, looking toward Amalek, foretold its utter destruction (the Amalek was the first of the nations, the first to war on Israel, Exodus 17:8, the foremost foe, the his latter end shall be that he perish for ever, the doom of utter destruction, the figure, the doom of Amalek, the first enemy of God's people, marked for destruction, the truth that God's avowed enemies perish, Amalek doomed, Exodus 17:14, 'I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek,' the figure of the perishing of the implacable foe of God and his people, the destruction of Amalek a figure of the end of all who set themselves against God's kingdom); Amalek the first to war on Israel (the foremost foe, Exodus 17:8) — the foe (Amalek first); 'his latter end shall be that he perish for ever' (Amalek doomed to perish, Exodus 17:14, the figure, the end of the implacable foe of God, the providence, the doom, the oracle against the Kenites to follow, vv.21-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And when he looked on Amalek, he took up his parable, and said, Amalek was the first of the nations; but his latter end shall be that he perish for ever
the oracle against Amalek, doomed to perish
Amalek, the first enemy of God's people, marked for utter destruction (Exodus 17:8, 17:14)
a figure of the end of all who set themselves against God's kingdom

schools.summary: The oracle on the Kenites, secure in the rock: 'and he looked on the Kenites, and took up his parable, and said, Strong is thy dwellingplace, and thou puttest thy nest in a rock' — Balaam observed the Kenites' secure dwelling, their nest set in the rock (the Strong is thy dwellingplace, the secure habitation, the thou puttest thy nest in a rock, the nest in the cliff, the seeming security, the figure, the seeming security of the Kenites, their nest in the rock, the truth that no earthly stronghold avails against God's judgment, vv.22, the figure of the false security of those who trust in their fastness rather than in God, the contrast with the true security of God's people in the Rock, the seeming strength that would yet be carried away captive, vv.22); the Kenites' secure dwelling (the strong habitation) — the dwelling (the secure place); 'thou puttest thy nest in a rock' (the nest in the cliff, the seeming security, the figure, the false security of the fastness, the providence, the security, the captivity to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he looked on the Kenites, and took up his parable, and said, Strong is thy dwellingplace, and thou puttest thy nest in a rock
the oracle on the Kenites, secure in the rock
the Kenites' seeming security in their stronghold, the false trust in the fastness rather than in God
the strength that would yet be carried away

schools.summary: The Kenite wasted and carried captive by Asshur: 'nevertheless the Kenite shall be wasted, until Asshur shall carry thee away captive' — despite their stronghold, the Kenites would be wasted and carried captive by Assyria (the the Kenite shall be wasted, the wasting, the decline, the until Asshur shall carry thee away captive, the captivity by Assyria, the figure, the false security of the fastness undone, the nest in the rock no defense against God's judgment, the truth that no stronghold avails when God brings the foe, the figure of the powerlessness of earthly security against the appointed judgment, the captivity by Asshur the appointed end, the seeming strength wasted, the contrast with the true and abiding security of those who hide in God the Rock); the Kenite wasted despite the stronghold (the decline) — the wasting (the Kenite undone); 'until Asshur shall carry thee away captive' (the captivity by Assyria, the appointed end, the figure, the fastness no defense against judgment, the providence, the captivity, the oracle against Asshur to follow, vv.23-24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Nevertheless the Kenite shall be wasted, until Asshur shall carry thee away captive
the Kenite wasted and carried captive by Asshur
the Kenites wasted despite their stronghold, the nest in the rock no defense against God's judgment
the seeming security undone, the powerlessness of earthly security against the appointed judgment

schools.summary: Balaam's lament at the judgments God would do: 'and he took up his parable, and said, Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!' — Balaam, foreseeing the great judgments and overturnings of the nations, cried out in awe, who could survive them (the he took up his parable, the last oracle, the Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!, the lament, the awe at the coming judgments, the figure, the awe before the judgments of God, the overturnings of the nations, the truth that when God arises to judge, the nations are shaken and few can stand, the figure of the terror of the day of God's judgment, 'who may abide the day of his coming?' Malachi 3:2, the lament of the seer before the great acts of God in the latter days, the shaking of the nations); Balaam's last oracle (the parable) — the oracle (the last parable); 'who shall live when God doeth this!' (the awe at the coming judgments, Malachi 3:2, the figure, the terror of the day of God's judgment, the providence, the lament, the fall of Asshur and Eber to follow, vv.24) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And he took up his parable, and said, Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!
Balaam's lament at the judgments God would do
the seer's cry of awe at the coming overturnings of the nations
the terror of the day when God arises to judge, few able to stand (Malachi 3:2)

schools.summary: The ships from Chittim afflicting Asshur and Eber: 'and ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, and shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber, and he also shall perish for ever' — Balaam foretold ships from the western lands (Chittim) afflicting Assyria and the Hebrews, and the final perishing of the last power (the ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, the western sea power, Greece or Rome, the shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber, the affliction of Assyria and the Hebrews, the he also shall perish for ever, the final doom of the last power, the figure, the rising and falling of the empires, the western power afflicting the east, all in turn perishing, the truth that the nations rise and fall under God's hand, the last empire too perishing, the figure of the succession of the kingdoms of the world, all passing away, that the kingdom of God's King may stand, the far prophecy of the latter days, the empires shaken, the King of vv.17 alone enduring); the western ships afflicting Asshur and Eber (the empires shaken) — the affliction (the western power); 'he also shall perish for ever' (the final perishing of the last power, the doom, the figure, all the empires passing, the King alone enduring, the providence, the prophecy, Balaam's departure to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, and shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber, and he also shall perish for ever
the ships from Chittim afflicting Asshur and Eber
the empires rising and falling under God's hand, all in turn perishing
that the kingdom of God's King alone may stand

schools.summary: Balaam and Balak parting each to his place: 'and Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place: and Balak also went his way' — the oracles ended, Balaam and Balak parted, each going his own way (the Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place, Balaam's departure, the Balak also went his way, Balak's departure, the figure, the parting of the diviner and the king, their schemes against Israel defeated, the truth that all their counsel against God's people came to nothing, the blessing standing, yet Balaam, though departing, would leave the counsel of Peor behind him, Numbers 31:16, the snare that the curse could not accomplish, the figure of the enemies' designs against God's people frustrated, the blessing unbroken, though the deeper snare of seduction would follow, ch.25); Balaam returning to his place (the departure) — the departure (Balaam gone); 'Balak also went his way' (Balak departing, the schemes defeated, Numbers 31:16, the figure, the counsel against Israel frustrated, the snare of Peor to follow, the providence, the parting, the sin at Peor to follow, ch.25) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place: and Balak also went his way
Balaam and Balak parting each to his place
the diviner and the king parting, their curse against God's people frustrated, the blessing unbroken
though the deeper snare of Peor would follow (Numbers 31:16)
